text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 23 April 2015 

Dr Sarah Bennett
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Sarah Bennett
Consultant Neurologist,  

Emma Clarke, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH-472839 NHS No. 9432185670  
12 Ferry Quay, Aberdeen, AB11 5RD

Thank you for your update and for coordinating the offshore work schedule with our service. I conducted a telephone review while she was onshore last week, with a further call planned when she returns from the next rotation. She works offshore on a 3-weeks-on/3-weeks-off rota, and her employer has agreed to facilitate private space for clinical calls when she is onshore. During the call, she was calm, coherent, and able to give a consistent account of recent events. There were no reports of new focal neurological symptoms. She continues to keep a simple diary to document episodes, sleep pattern, and stressors. 

Medication history was reviewed. Topiramate was discontinued on 20/Feb. Soon afterwards, she reported four seizures, one after a stressful day. She has had no seizures since then. She attributes improved stability to regular sleep offshore and avoiding double shifts when she is back on land. There were no reported nocturnal events, tongue biting, or injuries in this interval. Recovery times have returned to baseline and she denies prolonged confusion. 

We discussed triggers in the context of offshore work. She notes that transition days between sea and shore can be physically demanding and sometimes stressful; she has now arranged a staggered return to normal duties during the first 48 hours onshore. She denies alcohol binges and reports good hydration. Family members are aware of the plan to document any witnessed events. She has no current concerns about memory beyond occasional word-finding difficulty on fatigued days. 

She remains reluctant to attend for inpatient monitoring while at sea, but is open to telemetry during an onshore window if required. For now, given the recent pattern described above and her preference to minimise disruption to employment, I propose continuing telephone reviews aligned to her onshore weeks, with rapid escalation if there is any recurrence or change in semiology. She understands to contact us urgently if she experiences clusters, injury, prolonged recovery, or if colleagues note any new features. 

I will schedule the next telephone review for two days after she returns from the forthcoming offshore stint. If there is any immediate concern at your end, please let me know and we can bring this forward. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Soon afterwards, she reported four seizures... She has had no seizures since then.
Topiramate was discontinued on 20/Feb. Soon afterwards, she reported four seizures, one after a stressful day. She has had no seizures since then

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: 'Topiramate was discontinued on 20/Feb. Soon afterwards, she reported four seizures... She has had no seizures since then.' Clinic date is 23 April 2015. The period from 20 Feb to 23 Apr is approximately 2 months. She had 4 seizures soon after discontinuation and none thereafter within this interval. Since seizure freedom for at least 6 months is not established, we calculate frequency over this recent period as 4 seizures in about 2 months, yielding 4 per 2 months.
